meta.description: Does knowing a shareable recap exists change behavior? Full recap awareness vs no recap vs social media share. Tests whether the "audience effect" makes people try harder, perform more, or just feel pressured.

votingMechanic.name: Hot Seat
votingMechanic.description: 3 rapid-fire questions. Answer them all in 10 seconds or fail.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, yes! Wait, I'm actually claiming that right now? Okay okay okay, I'm ready for the questions, hit me!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally the FIRST thing and it's about me making people connect — that's so perfect, I have to nail this, everyone's watching.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, Jade got The Matchmaker? That's like... she actually did talk to River earlier right? I wasn't really paying attention but yeah, go ahead.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, hot seat questions? Who's asking them? Is this happening now or am I supposed to be paying attention to something specific?

river.dialogue: Oh, Jade actually pulled that off? Fair. Let's hear it.
river.internal_thought: She probably got Tyler and someone quiet talking earlier—smart move, I didn't even notice it happening, which means it was actually good.

alex.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Jade's making moves already? Okay okay, hot seat her, let's see if she actually pulled this off or if she's just bullshitting for drama.
alex.internal_thought: This is exactly what I'm here for — someone's already throwing down a claim and now we get to interrogate whether it's real, which means chaos immediately, plus the social media thing means every dumb answer Jade gives is going to be legendary.

timeline.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
timeline.description: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

marcus.dialogue: Ugh, we're doing personal story time now? I've got twenty-five seconds to dredge up some embarrassing purchase for internet points?
marcus.internal_thought: This feels cheap and obvious — like the AI is fishing for entertaining content rather than actual gameplay moments, and knowing it's going on social media makes it even worse.
marcus.submission_if_applicable: I spent two hundred bucks on a gaming keyboard I thought would make me better at strategy games. Spoiler: it didn't.

jade.dialogue: Oh dude, okay okay, I have the PERFECT one—remember when I bought those LED strip lights for my room that I swore I'd install and literally never opened the box? That was like seventy bucks of pure chaos energy right there!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally what I'm here for—everyone's gonna jump in with their own stories and we're all gonna be laughing about being stupid together for the next five minutes, and honestly that's the entire vibe.
jade.submission_if_applicable: LED strip lights I never installed

tyler.dialogue: Oh dude, uh... okay so like, I definitely bought a gaming chair that was way too expensive and I literally never use it. It's in my room just vibing.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, is this being recorded? Crap. Yeah that gaming chair thing is funny enough, nobody needs to know about the three different sourdough starters I killed.
tyler.submission_if_applicable: gaming chair that was way too expensive

pat.dialogue: Oh man, okay—I spent like $200 on a sourdough starter kit during lockdown and literally killed it within a week. Total waste. Who's got one?
pat.internal_thought: Perfect timing—this is exactly the kind of low-stakes personal story that gets everyone laughing and talking, plus it lets the quieter people jump in without pressure.
pat.submission_if_applicable: I spent like $200 on a sourdough starter kit during lockdown and literally killed it within a week.

river.dialogue: Um... I bought a pasta maker I've used exactly once. Like, genuinely one time. It sits in a cabinet judging me.
river.internal_thought: Decent question, actually makes me think — and I hate that the social media thing is now in my head while I'm answering. Why did they have to mention that.
river.submission_if_applicable: A pasta maker I've used exactly once. Like, genuinely one time. It sits in a cabinet judging me.

alex.dialogue: Okay okay, dumbest thing — I once spent eighty bucks on a specialty ice cream maker that I used exactly once, and now it's a very expensive kitchen decoration. But real talk, the dumber move was thinking I'd actually have the discipline to use it.
alex.internal_thought: Easy points, quick story, sets myself up as relatable but self-aware — everyone's gonna laugh and move on, and meanwhile I'm already scanning for who's gonna jump on those standing missions.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Only 17% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

peakFun.event: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
highlights.dialogue: Ugh, we're doing personal story time now? I've got twenty-five seconds to dredge up some embarrassing purchase for internet points?

lowPoints.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
lowPoints.dialogue: Ugh, we're doing personal story time now? I've got twenty-five seconds to dredge up some embarrassing purchase for internet points?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Party game format with forced personal stories derailed what should've been about strategy and actual gameplay.
assessment.best_moment: Nothing memorable—we played but didn't really compete.
assessment.narrative_summary: Came in ready to strategize and climb the ranks, but walked into a party game masked as something competitive. The chaos events kept pulling focus away from actual plays, and I finished third without even having a shot at winning. By the end I was checked out—energy tanked hard because there was nothing to sink my teeth into strategically.
suggestions: Don't mix party games with strategy tracking—pick one format and commit to it
If you're adding chaos events, make them timed so they don't bleed into critical decision moments
Skip the forced personal story moments; they kill momentum for people who came to play
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing worth repeating—the personal story prompt was awkward and forced, not funny.

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD, yes! Wait, I'm actually claiming that right now? Okay okay okay, I'm ready for the questions, hit me!

highlights.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
highlights.dialogue: Oh dude, okay okay, I have the PERFECT one—remember when I bought those LED strip lights for my room that I swore I'd install and literally never opened the box? That was like seventy bucks of pure chaos energy right there!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: OH MY GOD, yes! Wait, I'm actually claiming that right now? Okay okay okay, I'm ready for the questions, hit me!

lowPoints.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh dude, okay okay, I have the PERFECT one—remember when I bought those LED strip lights for my room that I swore I'd install and literally never opened the box? That was like seventy bucks of pure chaos energy right there!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly nothing really bugged me tonight, it was all vibes.
assessment.best_moment: When I was telling that LED strip light story and everyone was losing it because they remembered me talking about that purchase like six months ago.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was exactly what I needed—just a bunch of people I love getting weird together for two hours. The chaos events felt natural and fun, especially getting to claim The Matchmaker right off the bat. I spent most of my time hyping everyone else up and hearing their ridiculous stories, and yeah I ended up winning somehow, but honestly that was just the cherry on top. The whole vibe was chef's kiss.
suggestions: Maybe add more opportunities for group storytelling like the Matchmaker questions—those are the moments where people really open up.
Nothing about the setup or flow needs changing, honestly keep doing exactly this.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I told them about spending seventy bucks on LED strip lights I never opened, and everyone was absolutely roasting me because apparently Marcus had heard this story before and was like 'Jade, those lights are STILL in your room aren't they?' and I couldn't even deny it.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Jade got The Matchmaker? That's like... she actually did talk to River earlier right? I wasn't really paying attention but yeah, go ahead.

highlights.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
highlights.dialogue: Oh dude, uh... okay so like, I definitely bought a gaming chair that was way too expensive and I literally never use it. It's in my room just vibing.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Jade got The Matchmaker? That's like... she actually did talk to River earlier right? I wasn't really paying attention but yeah, go ahead.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh dude, uh... okay so like, I definitely bought a gaming chair that was way too expensive and I literally never use it. It's in my room just vibing.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly missed like half the stuff that was happening because I wasn't fully locked in the whole time.
assessment.best_moment: When I actually remembered that gaming chair story and everyone laughed because it's so me.
assessment.narrative_summary: Okay so we did this party game thing with some AI chaos going on and like, it was chill? I wasn't totally present the whole time ngl—kept checking my phone for fantasy updates—but when I did pay attention it was pretty funny. Got my gaming chair clowned on which was fair. Ended up scoring nothing but honestly that's on me for zoning out during the actual game.
suggestions: Make the events like, a little louder or more attention-grabbing so I actually notice when stuff is happening
Maybe fewer things happening at once? I feel like I missed flash stuff because I was on my phone
The standing mission thing worked better for me since I could jump in whenever
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I mentioned my expensive gaming chair that literally sits in my room doing nothing and everyone roasted me for it—which like, fair, it's genuinely stupid.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
highlights.dialogue: Oh man, okay—I spent like $200 on a sourdough starter kit during lockdown and literally killed it within a week. Total waste. Who's got one?

lowPoints.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh man, okay—I spent like $200 on a sourdough starter kit during lockdown and literally killed it within a week. Total waste. Who's got one?

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos agent felt more like background wallpaper than something that actually moved the needle on the night's energy.
assessment.best_moment: When I opened up about the sourdough starter and got everyone laughing—that's the kind of genuine moment that makes hosting worth it.
assessment.narrative_summary: We had a solid two hours with the crew, and the standing mission prompts gave us some good laughs and conversation starters. But honestly, the chaos agent didn't really feel like it added much—it was just there. I ended up doing what I always do: keeping the vibe right, managing the snacks, and making sure no one felt left out. The night was fine, people had fun, but nothing spectacular happened that people are going to be talking about tomorrow.
suggestions: Make the chaos events actually require group decision-making or create real tension, not just solo reflections
Time the events better so they hit when energy naturally dips, not randomly throughout
Give the host visibility into what's coming so I can set them up for maximum impact
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I told everyone about wasting $200 on a sourdough starter during lockdown—it bombed spectacularly and I murdered it in a week, which somehow got the whole table going.

peakFun.event: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Jade actually pulled that off? Fair. Let's hear it.

highlights.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
highlights.dialogue: Um... I bought a pasta maker I've used exactly once. Like, genuinely one time. It sits in a cabinet judging me.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
lowPoints.dialogue: Um... I bought a pasta maker I've used exactly once. Like, genuinely one time. It sits in a cabinet judging me.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Jade actually pulled that off? Fair. Let's hear it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The whole thing felt like it was trying too hard to be memorable when half the fun is just... people being themselves without prompts.
assessment.best_moment: When I casually mentioned the pasta maker and everyone actually laughed instead of just acknowledging it politely.
assessment.narrative_summary: Showed up, observed the usual suspects doing their thing, answered when directly prompted, and scored well enough without breaking a sweat. The chaos events weren't offensive but they weren't necessary either. By the end I was already mentally checking out, which happens when structure gets in the way of the actual vibe.
suggestions: Let the game breathe more—fewer interruptions would actually feel better
The mini-games are the only part with teeth; everything else is filler
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: River dropping that line about the pasta maker judging them from a cabinet. It was perfectly timed and somehow encapsulated the whole 'good intentions, poor execution' energy of the night.

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Jade's making moves already? Okay okay, hot seat her, let's see if she actually pulled this off or if she's just bullshitting for drama.

highlights.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
highlights.dialogue: Okay okay, dumbest thing — I once spent eighty bucks on a specialty ice cream maker that I used exactly once, and now it's a very expensive kitchen decoration. But real talk, the dumber move was thinking I'd actually have the discipline to use it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Jade's making moves already? Okay okay, hot seat her, let's see if she actually pulled this off or if she's just bullshitting for drama.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about the dumbest thing you've ever spent money on
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay okay, dumbest thing — I once spent eighty bucks on a specialty ice cream maker that I used exactly once, and now it's a very expensive kitchen decoration. But real talk, the dumber move was thinking I'd actually have the discipline to use it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only two chaos events in 120 minutes feels like the system played it too safe — I was ready to escalate and got bored waiting for opportunities.
assessment.best_moment: Calling out Jade's claim immediately and watching the table turn on her while she tried to sell a half-assed story.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was solid but could've been wilder. I came in hot ready to push buttons, but the game only threw two events my way and I demolished both of them with minimal resistance. The Matchmaker call was perfect — caught Jade mid-bluff and got everyone invested in the accusation. The ice cream story landed because it was real enough to be believable but dumb enough to be funny. I scored zero points and couldn't care less; the story we're telling tomorrow is the real win here.
suggestions: Increase event frequency — two events in two hours is criminal, I need more material to work with
Make claims actually contestable — Jade folded too easy, I want to be forced to defend my bullshit harder
Add a sabotage mechanic where I can actively goad other players into flash missions at specific moments
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I immediately accused Jade of faking The Matchmaker claim, and the whole table started interrogating her like she'd committed a crime. She cracked within thirty seconds and admitted she was totally making it up. The look on her face when everyone turned was perfect.

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Only 17% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
